title: App installed webhook
excerpt:
What feature are you requesting?

Somewhere in app configuration screen please add a section to configure a webhook that would be triggered on app installation. This webhook should deliver following information: client_…

posts.author: GTG (Grzegorz Tańczyk)
posts.cookedHtml:
• What feature are you requesting?

Somewhere in app configuration screen please add a section to configure a webhook that would be triggered on app installation. This webhook should deliver following information: client_id, account_id, slug, api access token for the app

Api access token should work for API requests on given slug/account_id accordingly to the Oauth settings.

  • How would this feature make your experience better?

My app consists only of a board view, and in order to make API requests from the app backend I need to perform the full Oauth for every user, which is not a great user experience.

I need to be able to make api requests from the backend in order to verify user access rights.

This API access token should allow user impersonification.

  • What is the use case or scenario that this feature could be helpful in?

Administrator installed my app. Users are entering the board view, the experience is seamless for them, they are not asked for Oauth authentication of the app. I as an app vendor don’t need to store api tokens for each user.

posts.author: dsilva (Daniel Silva)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hey @GTG - thank you so much for sharing with us. I’ll be sure to pass this along internally.

-Daniel

posts.author: allanl (Allan)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hello,

Just wondering if there is an update to this feature request. It would be incredibly useful for us too!

posts.author: Matias.Monday (Matias)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hello there @allanl,

This has been released: App life cycle events

Cheers,
Matias

posts.author: allanl (Allan)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi @Matias.Monday,

Thanks for the update!

From the docs, it seems that the request does not include the app access token for user impersonation. I am facing the same issue with @GTG. Otherwise, I would need to perform OAuth for every user, which would not provide a great user experience.

posts.author: Matias.Monday (Matias)
posts.cookedHtml:
@allanl hi!

The OAuth token gives you access to whatever that user (+ the app’s scopes) has access to.

Generally speaking, it makes sense for you to get one OAuth token per user.

If you do not do that, and you use one user’s token for your app, you may end up showing information that that user has access to, to a different user that should not see it. Or change something on behalf of a user that shouldn’t be able to change it (using that other user’s OAuth token).

posts.author: allanl (Allan)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi @Matias.Monday,

My app contains only a board view. Consider the following scenario:

  1. Administrator installs my app
  2. User navigates to board view
  3. My frontend app makes a call to the backend
  4. My backend can’t make an api call to monday, since there is no token available. I believe there is no shortLivedToken provided.
  5. User has to perform an oauth for my app backend to make an api call to monday

In this case, oauth must be performed for every user since there is no way for my backend to make an api call to monday. Do you know of any workaround?

posts.author: Matias.Monday (Matias)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hello again @allanl,

You can call the API via the SDK directly without needing to request the user to authorize the scopes to get an OAuth token.

If you would not like to use the SDK, then yes, each user should see the OAuth authorization screen once so that you can get the OAuth token specific to each user. Then once you have that, you don’t need to ask it again for that user. Then you would use the OAuth token of the specific user who is using your app each time (which you can know using the context from the SDK
